coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots from ginkgo leaves and application in biology
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Luyao
Wang, Xingxian
Fu, Zheng
Cui, Fengling
coredata.dc:description: The carbon dots have got much attention recently. Considering the environment protection, “green” materials are urgent. In this paper, nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were prepared through one-pot simple and convenient hydrothermal carbonization approach with “green” ginkgo leaves as the materials. The synthesized carbon dots were successfully doped with nitrogen and sulfur elements. The fluorescence intensities of carbon dots were rather stable in different pH and ionic strength. Furthermore, the “green” N,S-CDs were successfully applied in bioimaging as fluorescent probes due to its excellent optical properties and low cytotoxicity.

N,S-CDs
Bioimaging
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Toxicity of N,S-CDs and their bioimaging application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of carbon dots synthesized at (a) 160°C (b) 180°C (c) 200°C.
(a) TEM image of N,S-CDs (inset: corresponding HRTEM image) (b) The size distribution of N,S-CDs (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N,S-CDs (d) FT-IR spectrum of N,S-CDs.
Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells at (a) 405nm (b) 488nm excitation and KYSE410 cells at (c) 405nm (d) 488nm excitation labeled with N,S-CDs. Scale Bar: 30μm.

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots from ginkgo leaves and application in biology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of stable and green nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots from ginkgo leaves and application in biology.

The carbon dots have got much attention recently. Considering the environment protection, “green” materials are urgent. In this paper, nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) were prepared through one-pot simple and convenient hydrothermal carbonization approach with “green” ginkgo leaves as the materials. The synthesized carbon dots were successfully doped with nitrogen and sulfur elements. The fluorescence intensities of carbon dots were rather stable in different pH and ionic strength. Furthermore, the “green” N,S-CDs were successfully applied in bioimaging as fluorescent probes due to its excellent optical properties and low cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing development of nanomaterials, carbon dots have attracted attentions due to unique properties [1] of stable photoluminescence property, low toxicity, biological compatibility and environmental-friendliness. Carbon dots have stimulated the development of various smart nanosystems, such as biological imaging [2], biochemical detection [3], drug delivery [4], photocatalysis [5] and optical devices [6]. Due to their novel optical property, carbon dots are promising to be a competitive alternative for quantum dots based on heavy metal and organic dyes. Researchers have made great efforts to explore kinds of approaches to obtain fluorescent carbon dots, including laser ablation, arc discharge, pyrolysis, oxidation, electrochemical process, plasma, microwave radiation and microwave/ultrasonic passivation. However these methods suffer from the defects such as high energy consuming, high complexity of synthesis, high request of synthetic conditions, which limits the wider applications.

Herein, a simple one step hydrothermal carbonization was adopted to obtain the N,S-CDs employing ginkgo leaves as materials (Fig. S1). And there were two visible consequences in imaging compared with previous reports which only one.

Firstly, 2mL ginkgo leave juice and 23mL doubly distilled water were mixed thoroughly. Then the mixture was transferred into 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 160°C, 180°C, and 200°C for 12h respectively. Afterwards, the mixture was cooled to room temperature naturally. The large particles were removed from mixture through filtration with a 0.22μm filter membrane. Then the product was collected by centrifugation, and the supernatant was kept at 4°C for characterization and further use.
The cell viability was measured using the MTT assay.
The N,S-CDs as-prepared were applied to HeLa cells and KYSE410 cells imaging.

The N,S-CDs via one-step hydrothermal carbonization with gingko leaves as materials was reported. A comparison in the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots synthesized at different temperatures was made. From Fig. 1 , the carbon dots obtained at 180°C exhibited better fluorescence property than these at 160°C and 200°C. Thus 180°C was choose as the optimal reaction temperature. Fig.1b showed the typical absorption and PL spectra of N,S-CDs. The intensity of the PL depends on the number of particles excited at a particular wavelength. The highest PL intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 380nm, because of the largest number of particles being excited at 380nm. The maximum of emission band and emission fluorescence intensity changed with the increase of excitation wavelengths. When excitation wavelengths increased from 300–500nm, the emission wavelengths maximum was red-shifted from 453nm to 541nm. The emission intensity increased with excitation wavelength in the range of 300–380nm. When we further increased the excitation wavelength (from 380 to 500nm), the emission intensity decreased. The fact that the fluorescence emission wavelength and intensity depend on the excitation wavelength might be attributed to the energy traps on the surface of carbon dots [7]. The energy gap increases with the decrease in size of the carbon dots. A smaller size get excited at a lower wavelength and vice versa.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the N,S-CDs had a narrow size distribution and a good dispersion. The inset in Fig.2a showed the N,S-CDs had a lattice spacing of 0.202nm, which was consistent with the (102) lattice planes of graphitic carbon [8]. The average size of carbon dots was 2.22nm with distribution between 1nm and 4nm, which can be found from Fig.2b. The result of UV–Vis spectra analysis was displayed in Fig.2c. It can be observed that there was an obvious absorption peak in 275nm, which may be caused by the n-π∗ transition of CO bonds and the π-π∗ transition of CC bonds [9]. The component and surface groups of N,S-CDs were characterized by FT-IR and XPS. As illustrated in Fig.2d, the peak centered at 3400cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of OH or NH bonds [10]. The stretching vibrations of CN and CN were exhibited at 2360cm−1 and 1384cm−1 [11]. The peaks at 1630cm−1 and 1072cm−1 were attributed to CO and COC, respectively [10,12]. And the existence of sulfuric groups was confirmed by the stretching vibration of CS centered at 1121cm−1 [13]. As shown in Fig. S2, four major peaks of S, C, N and O obviously emerged at 167.5eV, 282.7eV, 400.0eV, and 532.0eV in the spectrum, indicating that CDs prepared here were mainly composed of S, 1.2%; C, 60.4%; N, 3.8%; O, 34.6%. A high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. S2b) confirmed the presence of CC (284.5eV), C-N/C-S (286.0eV), CO (287.1eV) and CO/CN (288.2eV) bonds [13]. This indicated that the as-prepared N,S-CDs were rich in hydrophilic groups on the surfaces, which was consistent with the corresponding FT-IR spectrum. The N1s spectrum (Fig. S2c) showed three peaks at 399.3eV, 400.1eV and 401.5eV, which were attributed to CNC, N(C)3 and NH bonds [14]. The two peaks at 168.8eV and 170.5eV (Fig. S2d) were attributed to CSO3 and CSO4
[15].

Then, the fluorescence stability of N,S-CDs was explored. Fluorescence spectroscopy was employed to assess the effect of different pH or ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs. The results revealed the excellent fluorescence stability of N,S-CDs. Fig. S3 showed that the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs does not change significantly with pH ranging from 2 to 10 and NaCl concentration changing. In addition, the interference of common metal ions to the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs was discussed. The result (Fig. S4) indicated that the influence could be neglected even with presence of high concentration of metal ions (50μM). Above all, the N,S-CDs from gingko leaves were with stable luminescence property. Moreover, pH, ionic strength and metal ions had little effect on the photoluminescence of N,S-CDs. Thus, the N,S-CDs was proper for imaging in cells or in vivo as fluorescence probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity of N,S-CDs and their bioimaging application
ce:section.ce:para: Before the cell imaging experiment, the toxicity of N,S-CDs by MTT method was evaluated firstly. Different concentrations of N,S-CDs were added to the cultured HeLa cells, and the cell viability was calculated through measuring the OD values after incubation. The result was shown in Fig. S5. With the concentration of N,S-CDs changing, there was no obvious decrease on the cell viability, which indicated that N,S-CDs was low toxic. Subsequently, the N,S-CDs were added into cultured HeLa cells and KYSE410 cells respectively. After 8h attachment, the N,S-CDs outside cells were washed off. Then Laser Scanning Confocal Microscope was used for capturing the fluorescence images of cells after incubation by N,S-CDs. From Fig. 3 , we observed bright blue (Fig.3a and c) and green (Fig.3b and d) inside cells with excitation at 405nm and 488nm respectively. It illustrated the N,S-CDs had entered into the cells. The results proved N,S-CDs from gingko leaves was appropriate for biological imaging as fluorescence probe. And the colour of cultured cells by N,S-CDs is selectable which gives rise to several visible consequences in imaging.

In summary, in contrast with other techniques, the method we adopted possesses numerous advantages, such as achievable synthetic condition, simple post-treatment processes and requirement. Furthermore, the materials of N,S-CDs we obtained is cheap and easy to access. What’s more, it’s green and environmentally friendly which accord with the development idea of nowadays society. The N,S-CDs synthesized have strong luminescence intensity and low cytotoxicity, are promising to have a broad space for further development and application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The supports from the Key Project of Henan Ministry of Education (14A150018), Key Programs of Henan for Science and Technology Development (142102310273), National Natural Science Foundation of China (30970696, 21172056), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Henan Province (2012IRTSTHN006), PCSIRT (IRT1061) and Instrumental Analysis Center of Tsinghua University are greatly appreciated.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2017.03.112.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
Ethylenediamine-assisted hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive biosensing and bioimaging
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
White light-emitting devices based on carbon dots’ electroluminescence
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury (II) ions
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
A biocompatible fluorescent ink based on water-soluble luminescent carbon nanodots
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate carbon dots from apple juice for imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells
N, S co-doped carbon dots with orange luminescence synthesized through polymerization and carbonization reaction of amino acids
Eco-friendly synthesis of shrimp egg-derived carbon dots for fluorescent bioimaging
Facile synthesis of oxygen and sulfur co-doped graphitic carbon nitride fluorescent quantum dots and their application for mercury(ii) detection and bioimaging
